summary: 18th U.S. president (1869-1877) and commanding general of the Union Army during the Civil War, born Hiram Ulysses Grant. Rose to prominence by forcing the "unconditional surrender" of Fort Donelson, capturing Vicksburg, and winning the Chattanooga Campaign, before being promoted to Lieutenant General and accepting Robert E. Lee's surrender at Appomattox Courthouse. As president he prosecuted the Ku Klux Klan and pursued an active foreign policy through Secretary of State Hamilton Fish, but his administration is remembered chiefly for scandals — the Credit Mobilier affair, the Whiskey Ring, Black Friday, and the Belknap kickback scheme. After leaving office he took a widely publicized world tour, was swindled out of his fortune by Ferdinand Ward, and wrote his acclaimed Personal Memoirs while dying of throat cancer, published by Mark Twain.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Grant is identified in giveaways: a Republican former Civil War general who became the 18th president, succeeding Andrew Johnson. His initials supposedly stand for his demand of "unconditional surrender," and his administration is proverbially remembered as corruption-plagued despite his own military reputation.

clues.clue: Republican president who was also a Union Civil War general, and served as president during Reconstruction
examples: name this Republican President who was also a Civil War general.
President during Reconstruction.

clues.clue: Succeeded Andrew Johnson as president
examples: identify this American president who succeeded Andrew Johnson and earned the nickname "Unconditional Surrender" during the Civil War.
After Missionary Ridge, Abraham Lincoln promoted this commanding officer... This general succeeded Andrew Johnson as President.

clues.clue: 18th president of the United States, presiding over a corruption-plagued administration
examples: name this corruption-plagued 18th president of the United States.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Unconditional Surrender" (playing on his U.S. initials) after his Civil War demand at Fort Donelson
examples: identify this American president who succeeded Andrew Johnson and earned the nickname "Unconditional Surrender" during the Civil War.

clues.clue: Won reelection in 1872 over Horace Greeley
examples: name this president who won reelection over Horace Greeley before his administration was embroiled in the Credit Mobilier scandal.

clues.clue: Only sitting U.S. president to give a deposition in a criminal trial
examples: This man was the only sitting US President to give a deposition in a criminal trial.

clues.clue: Declined to seek third terms in both 1876 and 1880 after the Credit Mobilier and Whiskey Ring scandals
examples: This politician declined to run for third terms in 1876 and 1880 after the Credit Mobilier and Whiskey Ring scandals.

works.name: Civil War Command
works.description: Grant's Civil War rise ran from a decisive western-theater victory at Fort Donelson through the sieges of Vicksburg and Chattanooga to overall command of the Union armies, culminating in Lee's surrender at Appomattox. His campaign was also marked by the notorious General Order No. 11 expelling Jews from his military department.

clues.clue: Forced the surrender of Fort Donelson (February 1862) from Simon Bolivar Buckner, demanding "unconditional surrender"; this brought him to Lincoln's attention
examples: This General's successful siege of Fort Donelson brought him to the attention of Abraham Lincoln. He ended the war as the commanding general of the entire Union army.
the Union commander whose forces captured this location was celebrated by the press for his demand of an "Unconditional Surrender."

clues.clue: Besieged and captured Vicksburg
examples: name this President who led Union troops at Vicksburg.
Bragg's troops found less success in the Chattanooga Campaign against the Union tropps of this general, who also seized Vicksburg.

clues.clue: Won the Chattanooga Campaign against Braxton Bragg, including the assault on Missionary Ridge; Lincoln then promoted him to Lieutenant General, a rank previously reserved for George Washington
examples: After Missionary Ridge, Abraham Lincoln promoted this commanding officer at the battle to Lieutenant General, a rank previously reserved for George Washington.
Bragg's troops found less success in the Chattanooga Campaign against the Union tropps of this general, who also seized Vicksburg.

clues.clue: Fought Robert E. Lee in the Overland Campaign, including the Battle of the Wilderness and the Battle of Spotsylvania Courthouse
examples: These two generals fought each other at Spotsylvania Courthouse. They met again at Appomattox Courthouse.

clues.clue: Ely S. Parker, a Seneca sachem, served as his military secretary and drafted the surrender terms at Appomattox; Grant later appointed Parker the first Native American Commissioner of Indian Affairs
examples: Parker worked as this general's secretary during the Civil War, for whom Parker drafted the surrender terms at Appomattox. This president later appointed Parker as the first Native Commissioner of Indian Affairs.

clues.clue: Accepted Robert E. Lee's surrender at Appomattox Courthouse to end the war; Lee arrived in full dress uniform while Grant wore a rough traveling/private's coat
examples: This man described a military adversary "dressed in full uniform" meeting him dressed in a "rough traveling suit" in an autobiography he wrote after being swindled out of his savings.
This victor at Fort Donelson and Vicksburg received Robert E. Lee's surrender to end the war.

clues.clue: Issued General Order No. 11, expelling Jews from the Department of the Tennessee, ostensibly to suppress the black-market cotton trade
examples: During the Civil War, this Union General issued General Order No. 11 expelling all Jews from the Department of the Tennessee.

works.name: Presidency: Reconstruction & Domestic Policy
works.description: As president, Grant used federal power to combat the Ku Klux Klan and suspended habeas corpus to enforce Reconstruction in the South, even as his administration was consumed by patronage disputes and the fallout from Gilded Age railroad and currency speculation.

clues.clue: Replaced Attorney General Ebenezer Hoar with Amos Akerman and created the office of Solicitor General (first held by Benjamin Bristow) to prosecute the Ku Klux Klan
examples: Ebenezer Hoar was replaced by Amos Akerman on the cabinet of this president, who created the office of Solicitor General to prosecute the KKK.

clues.clue: Suspended habeas corpus in several South Carolina counties to combat Klan violence
examples: this man's administration...also saw the suspension of habeas corpus in several South Carolina counties.

clues.clue: Secretary of War William Belknap took kickbacks from traders at Fort Sill; Carl Schurz led a breakaway "Liberal Republican" faction of Grant's party over such corruption
examples: This man's Secretary of War, William Belknap, took kickbacks from traders at Fort Sill, and Carl Schurz lead a breakaway faction of members of this man's party.

clues.clue: The Panic of 1873, triggered by the collapse of Jay Cooke's banking house, doomed the latter half of his presidency
examples: The Panic of 1873 doomed the presidency of this man, who earlier faced the Credit Mobilier and Whiskey Ring scandals.

clues.clue: Signed the Revenue Acts of 1872 and 1874 (the Anti-Moiety Acts), cutting the number of U.S. treasury agents after the Sanborn incident
examples: The Anti-Moiety Acts, which cut the number of US tax agents, were passed by this president as part of the Revenue Acts of 1872 and 1874.

works.name: Foreign Policy & World Tour
works.description: Secretary of State Hamilton Fish oversaw an ambitious foreign policy under Grant, including settling Civil War-era claims against Britain and an ultimately rejected bid to annex Santo Domingo; after leaving office Grant embarked on a much-publicized two-year world tour that took him to East Asia.

clues.clue: Secretary of State Hamilton Fish negotiated the Treaty of Washington (1871), settling the Alabama Claims with Britain
examples: His Secretary of State, Hamilton Fish, negotiated the Treaty of Washington to settle the Alabama Claims in 1870.
Hamilton Fish served as this man's Secretary of State. Both Black Friday and the Credit Mobilier scandal occurred during this man's administration.

clues.clue: Sought to annex Santo Domingo (the Dominican Republic); the treaty was rejected by the Senate after opposition led by Senator Charles Sumner, and Grant fired ambassador John Lothrop Motley in the aftermath
examples: This president fired John Lothrop Motley as ambassador to Great Britain in the aftermath of the Senate's rejection of his plan to annex Santo Domingo.
Name this US president whose failure to annex Santo Domingo became a major political embarrassment.

clues.clue: Sent an expedition under John Rodgers to the island of Gangwha, Korea, resulting in the Gangwha (Korean) Affair after failing to establish trade relations
examples: This leader ordered a military expedition led by John Rodgers to the island of Gangwha in an unsuccessful attempt to establish trade relations with Korea.
launched an expedition under John Rodgers to open trade with Korea that ended up firing on Korean forts in the Gangwha Affair.

clues.clue: Dispatched the USS Alaska to end the Grebo-Liberian War
examples: This man dispatched the USS Alaska to end the Grebo-Liberian War.

clues.clue: After his presidency, took a widely chronicled world tour: served as an envoy to Japan (at Prince Gong's urging) to help settle a Ryukyu Islands dispute and personally arbitrated Chinese and Japanese claims to the islands; journalist John Russell Young recounted the trip in Around the World with General Grant
examples: This politician was convinced by Prince Gong to serve as a personal envoy to Japan in an attempt to settle a dispute over the Ryukyu Islands, an event recounted in the journalist John Russell Young's account of spending two years abroad with this leader.
He arbitrated Chinese and Japanese claims to the Ryukus on his world tour.

clues.clue: During the same tour, the Maharajah of Jaipur tried to hospitably lose a game of billiards to him, but Grant was so bad he lost anyway
examples: According to one account, the Maharajah of Jaipur attempted to hospitably lose a game of billiards to this politician, but this politician was so terrible at billiards that he lost anyway.

works.name: Scandals of the Grant Administration
works.description: Grant's presidency is proverbial for its scandals: railroad and currency speculation schemes implicated members of his own party and cabinet, culminating in the Credit Mobilier affair and Whiskey Ring, and prompting sarcastic press coverage even before he left office.

clues.clue: The Credit Mobilier of America scandal — a railroad-finance company that paid bribes to Republican politicians, including Grant's running mate Henry Wilson (implicated for buying artificially inflated Union Pacific stock) — was revealed during his administration
examples: Credit Mobilier of America paid bribes to a number of Republican politicians during this president's scandal-plagued administration.
who was later implicated for buying artificially inflated Union Pacific stock during this man's presidency.

clues.clue: The Whiskey Ring — distillers bribed treasury agents to avoid liquor taxes; Grant's personal secretary Orville Babcock was implicated, and Grant appointed Benjamin Bristow to investigate
examples: He appointed Benjamin Bristow to investigate a scandal involving his personal secretary, Orville Babcock, who was complicit in the Whiskey Ring.
Orville Babcock became involved in the Whiskey Ring while serving as this man's secretary.

clues.clue: Black Friday (1869): Jay Gould and James Fisk attempted to corner the gold market, crashing the economy early in his presidency
examples: also during his tenure, Jim Fisk and Jay Gould attempted to corner the gold market on Black Friday.
Black Friday was an early incident during the presidential administration of this former Civil War general.

clues.clue: Secretary of the Interior Columbus Delano and Secretary of the Navy George Robeson were both caught taking bribes for contracts; Robeson was later lampooned in a cartoon of Grant as a trapeze artist holding him up
examples: His secretary of the interior, Columbus Delano, and his secretary of the navy, George Robeson, both got caught taking bribes for contracts.
This man was lampooned in a cartoon as a trapeze artist holding up men like George Robeson.

clues.clue: His administration was the first to suffer the Star Route scandal (postal-contract fraud)
examples: This first president's administration was the first to suffer from the Star Route scandal.

clues.clue: Evidence was stolen from a Washington D.A.'s safe (per informant Columbus Alexander) to discredit testimony during his administration
examples: Columbus Alexander had evidence taken from a safe in the Washington district attorney's office to discredit testimony during this man's administration.

clues.clue: After leaving office, financier Ferdinand Ward swindled Grant and his son out of their entire savings in a Ponzi scheme
examples: Twain's publishing house, a failure, had a rare success with the memoirs of this man. Ferdinand Ward scammed this man and his son out of all of their money in a Ponzi scheme.

works.name: Memoirs, Later Life & Legacy
works.description: Financially ruined by Ward's swindle and dying of throat cancer, Grant raced to complete his Personal Memoirs, which Mark Twain's publishing house issued to secure his family's finances; the book became a critically acclaimed 19th-century bestseller. His scandal-tarred reputation was later rehabilitated by biographers.

clues.clue: Wrote his Personal Memoirs (1885) while dying of throat cancer, focusing mostly on the Civil War rather than his scandal-filled presidency; Mark Twain's publishing house financed and published the memoirs to support Grant's family, and the book became a critically acclaimed, bestselling 19th-century memoir
examples: This President wrote his memoirs in 1885 while he was dying, and they were published by Mark Twain to support this man's family. He talked mostly about the Civil War and not his scandal-filled presidency.
Mark Twain briefly got out of debt by publishing this man's critically-acclaimed memoirs, which became one of the bestselling books of the 19th century in the United States.

clues.clue: Belva Lockwood, an early female attorney, petitioned Grant to be admitted to practice before the Supreme Court bar
examples: Belva Lockwood petitioned this president to allow her into the bar.

clues.clue: His 1880 visit to the Chautauqua Institution in New York helped spur its growth into a national lecture and education movement
examples: Chautauqua's growth took off after a visit from this US president.

clues.clue: Reportedly gave a guest, Horace Norton, an exploding cigar as a prank, which Norton kept on display for years
examples: This president supposedly gave Horace Norton an exploding cigar, which Norton kept on display for years.

clues.clue: His tarnished reputation was revived starting in the 1950s by biographer Lloyd Lewis; later biographers include Ron Chernow (2017) and Ronald C. White, who examined the Methodist faith of Grant's mother and his fascination with Mexico, while John F. Marszalek edited a scholarly edition of his memoirs and supervises his papers at Mississippi State University
examples: In the 1950s, Lloyd Lewis helped revive this president's reputation, which had been tarnished due to scandals such as the Whiskey Ring.
The biographer of Alexander Hamilton, Ron Chernow, published a biography about this man in 2017.

comprehensive_summary: Ulysses S. Grant (born Hiram Ulysses Grant, 1822-1885) was the 18th president of the United States (1869-1877) and, before that, commanding general of the Union Army. He rose to national attention in February 1862 by forcing the surrender of Fort Donelson from Simon Bolivar Buckner with a demand for "unconditional surrender" — supposedly the source of a nickname playing on his U.S. initials. He went on to besiege and capture Vicksburg, win the Chattanooga Campaign against Braxton Bragg (including the assault on Missionary Ridge, after which Lincoln promoted him to the rank of Lieutenant General, previously reserved for George Washington), and fight Robert E. Lee through the Overland Campaign's Battle of the Wilderness and Battle of Spotsylvania Courthouse. During the war he also issued the notorious General Order No. 11, expelling Jews from his Department of the Tennessee. His Seneca military secretary, Ely S. Parker, drafted the surrender terms Grant offered Lee at Appomattox Courthouse in 1865 — Lee arrived in full dress uniform to meet Grant in a rough traveling coat — ending the war; Grant later appointed Parker the first Native American Commissioner of Indian Affairs. As president, succeeding Andrew Johnson, Grant used federal power aggressively against the Ku Klux Klan, replacing Attorney General Ebenezer Hoar with Amos Akerman and creating the office of Solicitor General (first held by Benjamin Bristow), while suspending habeas corpus in parts of South Carolina. His Secretary of State, Hamilton Fish, negotiated the Treaty of Washington (1871) settling the Alabama Claims with Britain, while Grant's attempt to annex Santo Domingo collapsed in the Senate after opposition from Charles Sumner, costing ambassador John Lothrop Motley his job. He also sent USS Alaska to end the Grebo-Liberian War and dispatched an expedition under John Rodgers to Korea that ended in the failed, violent Gangwha Affair. His administration, however, is remembered above all for scandal: the Credit Mobilier railroad-finance affair (which implicated running mate Henry Wilson), the Whiskey Ring (which snared his personal secretary Orville Babcock and prompted Grant to appoint Bristow to investigate), the Black Friday gold-market corner by Jay Gould and James Fisk, kickback schemes involving Secretary of War William Belknap, Secretary of the Interior Columbus Delano, and Secretary of the Navy George Robeson, plus the Star Route postal-fraud scandal and the Panic of 1873 (triggered by the collapse of Jay Cooke's bank), which doomed the latter half of his presidency. Won reelection in 1872 over Horace Greeley, Grant declined to seek a third term in 1876 or 1880. After leaving office he took a much-publicized two-year world tour — chronicled by journalist John Russell Young in Around the World with General Grant — during which he served as an envoy to Japan at Prince Gong's urging to help settle a Ryukyu Islands dispute, personally arbitrated Chinese and Japanese claims to the islands, and reportedly lost a game of billiards to the Maharajah of Jaipur despite his host's attempt to lose politely. Financially ruined when Ferdinand Ward swindled him and his son in a Ponzi scheme, Grant raced to finish his Personal Memoirs while dying of throat cancer; Mark Twain's publishing house issued the book to support Grant's family, and it became a critically acclaimed, bestselling 19th-century memoir focused mostly on the Civil War rather than his scandal-ridden presidency. His reputation, badly tarnished by the scandals, began to be rehabilitated in the 1950s by biographer Lloyd Lewis and has been revisited since by Ron Chernow (2017) and Ronald C. White, among others.
recursive_suggestions: Robert E. Lee — Grant's Civil War adversary, shares the Overland Campaign and Appomattox surrender clues
Fort Donelson — recurring separate answerline distinct from Grant himself
Battle of Missionary Ridge / Chattanooga Campaign — recurring separate answerline (bonus parts) tied to Grant's promotion
Battle of the Wilderness and Battle of Spotsylvania Courthouse — recurring Overland Campaign answerlines
Credit Mobilier Scandal — recurring separate scandal answerline distinct from Grant
Whiskey Ring — recurring separate scandal answerline
Hamilton Fish — Grant's Secretary of State, has independent bonus-part presence (Treaty of Washington, Alabama Claims)
Ely S. Parker — Grant's military secretary and first Native American Commissioner of Indian Affairs, has his own bonus-part answerline
Mark Twain — publisher of Grant's memoirs; watch for confusion between Twain-answer and Grant-answer clues in shared bonuses
Jay Gould and James Fisk — Black Friday (1869) gold-market corner, distinct answerline
Charles Sumner — opposed the Santo Domingo annexation treaty
Braxton Bragg — Confederate commander Grant defeated at Chattanooga, has his own answerline
